description: This figure presents two mechanical property charts showing the compositional dependence of tensile and impact properties in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) illustrates the effect of aluminum content (3–10 wt%) on Fe-30Mn-xAl-(0.85,0.95)C steels water-quenched from 1150°C and aged at 550°C for 16 h, plotting ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (YS), Charpy U-notch impact energy (E_CUN), total elongation (TE), and reduction of area (δ) against Al concentration. Subfigure (b) displays the effect of carbon content (0.3–1.2 wt%) on Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC steels processed under similar conditions (water-quenched from 1050°C with dashed curves representing as-quenched state and solid curves representing aged condition at 550°C for 16 h). In (a), both UTS and YS remain relatively constant below 7 wt% Al (~800 MPa and ~600 MPa respectively for 0.95% C alloy) but increase sharply above 7 wt% Al, reaching ~1250 MPa UTS at 9–10 wt% Al, while impact energy and ductility peak at intermediate Al levels (~6–7 wt%) then decline. In (b), UTS and YS increase monotonically with carbon content, whereas impact energy and elongation maximize at approximately 0.7 wt% C (~130 J/cm² E_CUN and ~60% TE) before decreasing at higher carbon levels. These trends demonstrate that pronounced strengthening through fine dispersed κ′-carbide precipitation occurs only when Al exceeds 7 wt% and C exceeds 0.7 wt%, but excessive carbide volume fractions degrade ductility and toughness, establishing optimal compositional windows of Al 7–10 wt% and C ~0.7 wt% for balanced mechanical properties in age-hardenable austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys.
